coredata.dc:title: Engineering of onsite point-of-care testing of Fe3+ with visual ratiometric fluorescent signals of copper nanoclusters-driven portable smartphone
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ying
Jian, Jiayu
Sun, Baoguo
Wei, Yunlong
Pan, Daodong
Cao, Jinxuan
Shen, Yizhong
coredata.dc:description: Development of efficient point-of-care testing (POCT) toward heavy metals is an effective way to establish warning-early mechanisms against environmental and food safety risks, safeguarding public health. Here, we engineered an intelligent smartphone-based POCT platform for detecting of Fe3+ in environmental and food matrices. We firstly synthesized bovine serum albumin-protected copper nanoclusters (BSA-CuNCs) to assemble [Ru(bpy)3]2+ for obtaining dual-emission BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ via electrostatic attraction. However, Fe3+ triggered the fluorescence quenching of BSA-CuNCs at 452 nm through aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) effect, while there was a negligible influence on the emission of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ at 624 nm, obtaining a ratiometric fluorescent responding to Fe3+. By integrating into a smartphone-based gadget, we achieved fluorescence color varies from cyan-blue to red, which were transduced by the Color Picker APP into the RGB values that displayed linear relationship to Fe3+ concentration among 0.0 – 15.0 μM (R 2 = 0.995), with a detection limit of 0.086 μM and a response time within 3.0 min. Notably, this portable platform has been used to gauge Fe3+ in tap water and spirits, whose results agreed with ICP-MS analysis, proving its practicability for onsite determination of Fe3+ in safeguarding environmental and food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Point-of-care testing
Ratiometric fluorescence
Smartphone
Fe3+
Environmental and food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence response of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ to Fe3+
Visual assay of Fe3+ on BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+-assisted smartphone-based POCT platform
Onsite visual POC assay of Fe3+ in actual samples
Characterization of BSA-CuNCs with cyan-blue emission
Ratiometric fluorescent responding of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ to Fe3+
Response mechanism of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ to Fe3+
Smartphone-integrated POCT platform for onsite determination of Fe3+
Onsite visual POC assay of Fe3+ in actual samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research Program of China ( 2016YFD0401502 ), the Modern Agricultural Technical Foundation of China ( CARS-42–25 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22176047 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China ( PA2021GDSK0101 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the BSA-CuNCs. (A) TEM graph, (B) Size distribution, (C) XPS survey spectrum, (D) Cu 2p, (E) O 1 s, and (F) C1s spectra of the BSA-CuNCs. Inset of Fig. 1A is the HRTEM graph of the BSA-CuNCs. (G) FT-IR spectra of BSA and BSA-CuNCs. (H) UV-Vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of the BSA-CuNCs, inset corresponded to the graphs of BSA-CuNCs before (left) and after (right) 365 nm UV light excitation. (I) Emission spectrum of BSA-CuNCs at excitation wavelengths from 350 nm to 390 nm.

Characterization of the assembly BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+. (A) ζ-potential, (B) FT-IR, (C) UV-Vis absorption, and (D) Emission spectra of BSA-CuNCs (1.3 mM), [Ru(bpy)3]2+ (15.0 μM), BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ (15.0 μM) in D.I. water. Inset of Fig. 2D is the photographs (from left to right) of BSA-CuNCs, [Ru(bpy)3 2+], and BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ under a 365 nm UV light.

Response of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ (15.0 μM) toward Fe3+.(A) Emission spectra of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ following incubation with 0.0–250.0 μM Fe3+ within 3.0 min (B) CIE chromaticity diagram of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ answering to Fe3+ as shown in Fig. 3A. (C) Photographs of the BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ reacting with Fe3+ over a concentration range of 0.0–250.0 μM for 3.0 min with (left) and without (right) excitation with a 365 nm UV light. (D) The relationship between the F 452 nm/F 624 nm of the BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ and Fe3+ in the concentration range of 0.0–250.0 μM. (E) Plot of F 452 nm /F 624 nm of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ versus the concentrations of Fe3+ from 1.0 to 50.0 μM (F) Emission spectra of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ after reaction with these ions within 3.0 min. The concentration of Fe3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ag+, or Zn2+ was 100.0 μM, and others was 1.0 mM.

Profiling the response mechanism of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ toward Fe3+. (A) Fluorescence lifetime curves of BSA-CuNCs in the absence and presence of Fe3+. (B) Temperature-dependent Stern-Volmer plots of the F 0/F of BSA-CuNCs versus Fe3+ concentrations. (F 0 and F expressed the fluorescence intensities of BSA-CuNCs at 452 nm with and without reaction with Fe3+). (C) UV-Vis absorption spectra of Fe3+, BSA-CuNCs, and BSA-CuNCs following incubation with Fe3+. (D) Emission spectra of BSA-CuNCs (black line), BSA-CuNCs with 100.0 μM Fe3+ added (red line), BSA-CuNCs with 100.0 μM Fe3+ added, then mixing with 150.0 μM EDTA (blue line), and BSA-CuNCs with 150.0 μM EDTA added (magenta line). TEM images of BSA-CuNCs before (E) and after (F) incubation of 100.0 μM Fe3+.

Integration of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ with smartphone to develop POCT platform for sensing Fe3+. (A) Photograph of the POCT platform and Fe3+ concentration-dependent fluorescence imaging of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ on this POCT platform. Fe3+ (from left to right): 0.0, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0 10.0, 15.0 25.0, 50.0 75.0, 100.0, 150.0, and 250.0 μM, respectively. (B) Plot of the R/B versus the concentrations of Fe3+ among 0.0 – 15.0 μM. (C) Comparison of the R/B and corresponding color (inset graph) of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ answering to Fe3+ and some interfering ions on the POCT platform. The concentration of Fe3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ag+ and Zn2+ was 100.0 μM, and others was 1.0 mM.

Schematic of the engineering of onsite point-of-care testing of Fe3+ with visual ratiometric fluorescent signals of copper nanoclusters-driven portable smartphone. (A) Diagram of Fe3+-triggered ACQ of BSA-CuNCs in dual-emission BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ in ratiometric response to Fe3+. (B) Diagram of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+-integrated smartphone-assisted portable platform for onsite visual assay of Fe3+.
Portable POCT platform for visual monitoring of Fe3+ in actual samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Engineering of onsite point-of-care testing of Fe3+ with visual ratiometric fluorescent signals of copper nanoclusters-driven portable smartphone

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food & Biological Engineering, Key Laboratory for Agricultural Products Processing of Anhui Province, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food & Biological Engineering, Key Laboratory for Agricultural Products Processing of Anhui Province, Hefei University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food & Biological Engineering, Key Laboratory for Agricultural Products Processing of Anhui Province, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei, 230009, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Author Contributions: Y. Wang and J. Jian contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of efficient point-of-care testing (POCT) toward heavy metals is an effective way to establish warning-early mechanisms against environmental and food safety risks, safeguarding public health. Here, we engineered an intelligent smartphone-based POCT platform for detecting of Fe3+ in environmental and food matrices. We firstly synthesized bovine serum albumin-protected copper nanoclusters (BSA-CuNCs) to assemble [Ru(bpy)3]2+ for obtaining dual-emission BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ via electrostatic attraction. However, Fe3+ triggered the fluorescence quenching of BSA-CuNCs at 452 nm through aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) effect, while there was a negligible influence on the emission of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ at 624 nm, obtaining a ratiometric fluorescent responding to Fe3+. By integrating into a smartphone-based gadget, we achieved fluorescence color varies from cyan-blue to red, which were transduced by the Color Picker APP into the RGB values that displayed linear relationship to Fe3+ concentration among 0.0 – 15.0 μM (R 2 = 0.995), with a detection limit of 0.086 μM and a response time within 3.0 min. Notably, this portable platform has been used to gauge Fe3+ in tap water and spirits, whose results agreed with ICP-MS analysis, proving its practicability for onsite determination of Fe3+ in safeguarding environmental and food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric ions (Fe3+), an indispensable element in biological systems, play vital roles in catalyzing the synthesis of hemoglobin, cytochromes and various enzyme proteins, oxygen transportation, as well as hematopoiesis [1–4]. When Fe3+ in the human body deviates from the normal level, it will induce serious disorders of cell metabolism and lead to various diseases, like hypoferric anemia [5], hepatic injury [6], Alzheimer's disease [7], pigmentation [8], and even cancer [9]. In recent decades, excessive Fe3+ has been continuously released into the environment due to the development of industry and anthropogenic processes, causing serious water and soil pollution, which posed a huge threat to environmental safety. Currently, the level of Fe3+ has been regarded as a critical indicator for evaluating the quality of water [10–12]. Furthermore, superfluous Fe3+ in environmental (e.g., water & soil) can affect food quality or cause food contamination and eventually threaten public health through food chains [13,14]. Therefore, it is still very desirable to develop an efficient, portable and easy-to-operation point-of-care testing (POCT) platform for achieving the effective early warning against excessive Fe3+ in environmental and food matrices.

Recently, the built-in camera and information processing function have endowed smartphone to be a popular candidate for developing portable optical sensors, achieving the POC assays of different environmental and food contaminations [15,16]. Particularly, the fluorescence signals-integrated smartphone possesses a higher sensitivity than that of conventional eye-naked colorimetry, which is more suitable for visual assays of tracing contaminants in real samples [17–19]. Given all that, tremendous efforts have been praised in exploring reliable fluorescence signals-integrated smartphone-assisted portable sensing platforms for real-time Fe3+ detection. For instance, Zhao et al. engineered the MIL-53(Fe)-(OH)2 integration with smartphone for onsite monitoring of Fe3+ by exploiting the specific binding of Fe3+ with hydroxyl to obtain a response of fluorescence signal quenching [20]. Lately, Shen et al. designed the N-connected disalicylaldehyde H2Qj1 with the excited-state intramolecular proton transfer character integrated with the smartphone for carrying out the onsite detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in tap water [21]. However, most reported smartphone-assisted portable sensing platforms for analysis of Fe3+ primarily incorporated single emission that were susceptible to interference from target-independent factors, as well as the testing process remained dependent on dark environmental conditions and handheld UV lamp. In contrast, the ratiometric fluorescent strategies generally exhibited the built-in self-calibration function for reducing/evading the false-positive readout caused by the target-independent factors, thereby expanding the dynamic monitoring range in complicated environmental and food matrices while ensuring high analytical accuracy [22–25]. Therefore, it is a promising choice to design a ratiometric fluorescent strategy-assisted smartphone as a highly integrated, instrument-free, and miniaturized portable POCT platform to realize accurately real-time visual Fe3+ monitoring in environmental and food matrices.

Nowadays, metal nanoclusters have drew much attention in sensing due to their photostability and biocompatibility that are superior to conventional quantum dots and organic dyes [26,27]. At present, the reported metal nanoclusters included silver nanoclusters, gold nanoclusters, and copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) [28,29]. Among them, CuNCs have ignited more research interests in developing various fluorescence sensors due to their low cost and diversified precursors [30,31]. Inspired by these, we here integrated functionalized-CuNCs into smartphone to develop a portable POCT platform for achieving ratiometric fluorescence signals-driven onsite visual monitoring of Fe3+ in a precise, reliable and cheap way. To this end, we synthesized cyan-blue fluorescent BSA-CuNCs, which possessed a maximum emission peak at 452 nm. The negative BSA-CuNCs could bind with the positively fluorophore [Ru(bpy)3]2+ to prepare dual-emission assembly of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+. Driven by the stronger chelation of Fe3+ toward BSA-CuNCs, the emission at 452 nm was apparently quenched by Fe3+-triggered aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) effect. On the contrary, the emission from [Ru(bpy)3]2+ inside BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ at 624 nm remained almost unchanged. On this basis, a ratiometric fluorescence response of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ toward Fe3+ was proposed. By integrating into a smartphone-based gadget, the fluorescence colors vary from cyan-blue to red associated with Fe3+ concentration were captured, which could be converted into RGB values to obtain a linear relationship of R/B versus Fe3+ concentration among 0.0 – 15.0 μM (R 2 = 0.995) through the Color Picker APP ( Scheme 1). Notably, this portable POCT platform exhibited an available limit of detection (LOD) of 0.086 μM and a rapid testing time of 3.0 min responding to Fe3+ in tap water and commercial spirits with satisfactory recoveries, which were almost consistent with the results on ICP-MS, demonstrating its wide application future in environmental health and food security assessment.

We synthesized the BSA-CuNCs by the reported way with some modifications [32]. We first dissolved 150.0 mg CuSO4 ·5 H2O and 336.0 mg NaKC₄H₄O₆ in 60.0 mL D.I. water, then the pH of solution was regulated to 12.0 with 1.0 M NaOH to obtain the Cu2+ stock solution. The Cu2+ stock solution (10.0 mM) was added to the BSA solution (10.0 mg/mL) and mixed for 5.0 min. Then put 8.0 mL 30.0% H2O2 into the mixed solution with whisking for 3.0 min, and irradiated in the microwave for 120 s at 250.0 W. The obtained solution was purified by ultrafiltration with a Millipore (10.0 kDa) at 5000 rpm for 15.0 min. The solution finally obtained after standing for 24.0 h and preserved in refrigerator at 4.0 ℃ for later use. BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ was prepared through blending the BSA-CuNCs (3.2 mM, determined by the concentration of Cu2+ used to synthesize BSA-CuNCs) and Ru(bpy)3Cl2.6 H2O (48.0 μM) in a volume ratio of 2: 3 at room temperature for 2.0 h. BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ concentration was 15.0 μM, depended on the concentration of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ assembled on the BSA-CuNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence response of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 120.0 μL of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ was mixed with 80.0 μL of Fe3+ for 3.0 min. Fluorescence spectra upon 365 nm excitation wavelength were then collected. In addition, the selectivity was conducted via adding other interfering substances to substitute for Fe3+ adopting the same method as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual assay of Fe3+ on BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+-assisted smartphone-based POCT platform
ce:section.ce:para: To realize fluorescence imaging of Fe3+ on the smartphone-based POCT platform, we first dropped the solution (50.0 μL) of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ reaction with Fe3+ for 3 min into the filter paper strip. Subsequently, a 365 nm UV lamp was lightened up utilizing the mobile power supply to excite the filter paper, the images of the filter papers were taken with a smartphone and turned into RGB values through the Color Picker APP for Fe3+ detection. Likewise, the images of filter paper-loaded 50.0 μL of the mixture containing BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ and Fe3+ or other interferential ions (i.e., Cu2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Br-, F-, NO2 -, NO3 -, SO4 2-) were captured and then turned into RGB values to evaluate the selectivity of the POCT platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Onsite visual POC assay of Fe3+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: We employed the portable platform to measure the Fe3+ in spirits and tap water to verify its practicability. Briefly, 60.0 μL of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ was first incubated with 40.0 μL spirits or tap water samples containing 2.5 μM, 5.0 μM, 10.0 μM Fe3+ in pH 6.0 PBS buffer for 3.0 min, respectively. Then follow the operation steps in Section 2.3 to quantify Fe3+ concentration in real samples. All of these samples were further gauged through ICP-MS to validate the accuracy of this POCT platform for Fe3+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of BSA-CuNCs with cyan-blue emission
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 A, the transmission electron microscope (TEM) graph demonstrated the BSA-CuNCs was the regularly spherical with an average core size of approximately 2.0 nm (Fig. 1B). Further, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) graph exhibited it possessed the crystal lattice spacing of 2.16 Å, belonging to the (111) diffraction plane of metallic Cu (inset of Fig. 1 A). These results strongly indicated that the BSA-CuNCs conformed to a nanocluster-like structure [33]. Moreover, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to study the surface element and valence states of Cu in BSA-CuNCs. Fig. 1 C was the survey spectrum indicated that BSA-CuNCs was composed of the typical elements such as C, O, N, S, and Cu. And two obvious peaks at 932.4 and 952.0 eV in Cu 2p spectrum were resulting from the Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 (Fig. 1D), which indicated the presence of Cu (0) or Cu (I). Notably, there was no satellite peak observed at 942.0 eV confirmed that no divalent Cu in BSA-CuNCs [34]. Apart from these, the peaks at 531.6 and 532.7 eV of O 1 s spectra were primary due to the formation of C-O and CO (Fig. 1E) [35]. Meanwhile, the peaks in the C 1 s spectrum were corresponded to CC at 284.7 eV, C-O at 286.2 eV, and C-OO at 288.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 1 F) [36]. To verify the successful assembly of BSA on CuNCs surface, the FT-IR analysis was conducted. As depicted in Fig. 1 G, the peaks of BSA-CuNCs at 1637 and 1518 cm−1 were contributed to the stretching vibration of C-O and COO-, showing the same position as alone BSA. These results coincided well with the analysis from the above C 1 s and O 1 s spectra and revealed that BSA was triumphantly immobilized on the surface of CuNCs.

Having synthesized BSA-CuNCs, we then explored its optical features. As shown in Fig. 1H, there was no major surface plasmon bands among 400 – 500 nm, indicating that there was no large copper nanoparticle, further proving the successful synthesis of BSA-CuNCs [37]. Fig. 1I demonstrated that 365 nm is the most optimal excitation wavelength of this BSA-CuNCs, which exhibited a cyan-blue fluorescence image upon 365 nm UV lamp. What’s more, the BSA-CuNCs exhibited an excitation-dependent feature, probably attributable to its non-uniform size distribution which contained a collection of quantized energy states, resulting in that each excitation wavelength corresponded to a specific energy level of emission [38]. Besides, we measured that the quantum yield of BSA-CuNCs was 17.71% with quinine sulfate as the reference (Note S1 & Table S1). Notably, we noticed that the BSA-CuNCs were stable in aqueous solution by measuring its fluorescence over a half month (Fig. S1A & B). Meanwhile, we also observed that this BSA-CuNCs had good salt solution stability, owing to its unchanged fluorescence with the increased sodium chloride concentration in the system (Fig. S1C & D). Moreover, the fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs decreased when the pH value was less than 6.0, but it had no evident changed as the solution turned alkaline (Fig. S1E & F).

The BSA-CuNCs with stable fluorescence aroused us to explore its feasibility in visual POC assays. It was reported that the ratiometric fluorescent strategy-integrated smartphone is a promising POCT platform. To this end, we firstly examined the surface zeta potential of BSA-CuNCs to be − 21.36 mV, which could be increased to − 16.10 mV after assembly of positively charged [Ru(bpy)3]2+ by electrostatic action ( Fig. 2A), yielding a dual-emission assembly BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+. To prove this point, we explored the FT-IR spectra of BSA-CuNCs, [Ru(bpy)3]2+, and BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+. Fig. 2B demonstrated both the characteristic peaks of BSA-CuNCs and [Ru(bpy)3]2+ appeared in the FT-IR spectra of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ [39,40]. Moreover, we found that BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ possessed a characteristic absorption peak of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ at 452 nm (Fig. 2C) [41]. Meanwhile, BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ displayed two different emission peaks at 452 nm and 624 nm upon 365 nm excitation, which were ascribed to the fluorescence emissions of BSA-CuNCs and [Ru(bpy)3]2+, respectively (Fig. 2D). Correspondingly, BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ showed a similar cyan-blue fluorescence color to BSA-CuNCs but distinctly different from [Ru(bpy)3]2+ (Inset of Fig. 2D). To intuitively confirm the successful assembly of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+, we then performed the dynamic light scattering (DLS) and atomic force microscope (AFM) characterizations. The results displayed the obvious increase of the hydrodynamic diameter together with the height of BSA-CuNCs before and after assembling [Ru(bpy)3]2+ (Fig. S2). All above results strongly proved the successful assembly of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+. Notably, the fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ remained for 7 days in a 4.0 °C refrigerator (Fig. S3A & B), and also remained basically unchanged when stored in different concentrations of sodium chloride solution (Fig. S3C & D). The above suggested that BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ possessed superior stability which would broaden its application in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent responding of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Having obtained the assembly of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+, the fluorescent change of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ in response to Fe3+ was further examined. Before performing this, we firstly optimized the experimental conditions, including pH, incubation time, the concentration ratio of BSA-CuNCs to [Ru(bpy)3]2+, and BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ concentration. In this aspect, we measured the pH effect on the F 452 nm/F 624 nm of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ in response to Fe3+ in PBS buffer among a pH from 4.5 to 8.0, observing a minimum F 452 nm/F 624 nm at pH 6.0 (Fig. S4A), indicating that 6.0 was the optimal pH for BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ response to Fe3+. Meanwhile, the reaction time-dependent F 452 nm/F 624 nm of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ after reaction with Fe3+ for 10.0 min was tested. The results demonstrated that the F 452 nm/F 624 nm descended rapidly and reached a plateau in 3.0 min (Fig. S4B), implying that the optimal response time was 3.0 min, agreeing well with the demands of POC assays. In addition, as the concentration ratio of BSA-CuNCs to [Ru(bpy)3]2+ during synthesis of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ decreased, the F 452 nm/F 624 nm of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ gradually decreased and reached a minimum at the concentration ratio of 44.4 (Fig. S4C). Therefore, the optimal composition ratio of BSA-CuNCs to [Ru(bpy)3]2+ for constructing the BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ responding to Fe3+ was 44.4. Likewise, we also found that 15.0 μM was the optimal concentration of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ for Fe3+ response, owing to the minimum F 452 nm/F 624 nm of 15.0 μM BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ after reaction with Fe3+ (Fig. S4D).

Next, we evaluated the responding of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ to Fe3+ upon the optimal terms. As depicted in Fig. 3A, with the concentration of Fe3+ increasing from 0.0 to 250.0 μM, the emission intensity of BSA-CuNCs at 452 nm decreased apparently, while the emission peak at 624 nm of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ showed no obvious change, thereby emerging a typical ratiometric response. Correspondingly, the reduction of the F 452 nm/F 624 nm brought about a pronounced variations in fluorescence color from cyan-blue to red, which was in accordance with the theoretical CIE coordinate analysis (Fig. 3B & C), facilitating the development of a Fe3+-responsive eye-naked visual assay-based the ratiometric signals-driven the color change under excitation of UV lamp. By plotting the F 452 nm/F 624 nm versus Fe3+ concentration, we proposed a good linear relationship F 452 nm/F 624 nm = 2.62 – 0.0166 [Fe3+] (R 2 = 0.992) in the range of 1.0 – 50.0 μM. And the LOD was as low as 0.092 μM (Fig. 3D & E). As compared with other Fe3+-responsive fluorophores, BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ displayed a better sensitivity (Table S2).

It is well-known that the selectivity is also a pivotal parameter for a analytical technique. Regarding to this, the response ability of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ toward Fe3+ over other ions such as Cu2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Br-, F-, NO2 -, NO3 -, SO4 2-, and CO3 2- was next estimated by comparing the F 452 nm/F 624 nm after reaction with the ion over 3.0 min. The results revealed that only Fe3+ could trigger the remarkable decrease in the F 452 nm/F 624 nm, while other ions displayed negligible effect on the F 452 nm/F 624 nm (Fig. 3 F & S5). These facts strongly revealed that BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ presented a high selectivity for Fe3+ against these interfering ions, and met the need for selective analysis of Fe3+ in actual samples. The excellent selectivity of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ to Fe3+ may be owing to higher charge density and stronger electron absorption capacity of Fe3+ compared to other metal ions [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. S6A, the fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. By plotting the fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs at 452 nm versus Fe3+ concentration, a regression equation of F = 810.66 – 8.18 [Fe3+] (R 2 = 0.978) was proposed among 1.0 – 50.0 μM with a LOD of 0.18 μM (Fig. S6B & C). Meanwhile, we noticed that the emission of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ at 624 nm almost remained after incubation with Fe3+, suggesting that [Ru(bpy)3]2+ was not responsive to Fe3+ (Fig. S7). Given these characteristics, [Ru(bpy)3]2+ was selected as the internal reference to combine with BSA-CuNCs to assemble a ratiometric nanoprobe BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ for Fe3+ assay.

In this case, we next explored the mechanism for Fe3+-triggered BSA-CuNCs fluorescence quenching. As shown in Fig. S8, there was an insignificant optical overlap between BSA-CuNCs emission and of Fe3+ absorption. Meanwhile, there was a negligible change in the fluorescence lifetime of BSA-CuNCs before (τ0 = 3.97 ± 0.15 ns) and after (τ = 3.96 ± 0.11 ns) reaction with Fe3+ ( Fig. 4A), completely ruling out FRET process in Fe3+-triggered BSA-CuNCs fluorescence quenching [27,43,44]. Generally, the effect of incubation temperature on the fluorescence quenching constant (K sv) was usually employed as an effective technique to explore the fluorescence quenching process. The K sv at different incubation temperatures could be calculated by exploiting the classical Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)).
(1)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
sv
[ Q ]
Where F
0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs before and after the reaction with Fe3+. [Q] referred to the Fe3+ concentration. As presented in Fig. 4B, the K sv value was negatively correlated with the incubation temperatures, suggesting that Fe3+-triggered BSA-CuNCs fluorescence quenching was static binding process.

It has been reported that the coordination bond formed between Fe3+ and the carboxyl groups in BSA-CuNCs easily transferred electrons from the BSA-CuNCs to Fe3+, which would hinder the electron transport between Cu-N on the surface of BSA-CuNCs, resulting in the static fluorescence quenching of BSA-CuNCs [45]. Consequently, we investigated the absorption spectra of BSA-CuNCs reacting with and without Fe3+. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 C, there was a significant absorption red-shift of BSA-CuNCs after incubation with Fe3+, indicating that BSA-CuNCs might combine with Fe3+ to generate CuNCs-Fe3+ complex, which confirmed the static quenching mechanism. To obtain more details about the interaction between Fe3+ and BSA-CuNCs, we added ethylenedinitrilo tetra acetic acid (EDTA), a strong chelating agent for metal ions, to the mixture of BSA-CuNCs and Fe3+, the fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs quenched by Fe3+ was partially restored after EDTA was introduced (Fig. 4D). This could be attributed to the stronger coordination between EDTA and Fe3+, forming more stable complex than CuNCs-Fe3+, which removed part of Fe3+ exposed to the surface of BSA-CuNCs and allowed partial fluorescence recovery. The results further illustrated the occurrence of static quenching in Fe3+-triggered BSA-CuNCs fluorescence decrease [29].

As previously reported, Fe3+ could bind to ligands containing orthoquinones for forming coordination complexes between the electrophilic oxygen atoms of the carbonyl group and the empty d orbitals of Fe3+, resulting in a reduction in the gap between adjacent nanoclusters to produce large aggregates, which could lead to fluorescence quenching [46]. In addition, the zeta potential of BSA-CuNCs increased after the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. S9), from which we speculated that Fe3+ could bind to BSA-CuNCs through electrostatic interactions and eventually led to the aggregation of BSA-CuNCs resulting in fluorescence quenching. Based on these, we finally performed the TEM characterization to observe BSA-CuNCs morphologies before and after incubation with Fe3+. As depicted in Fig. 4E & F, Fe3+ could trigger BSA-CuNCs to be aggregation for forming larger nanoparticles than free BSA-CuNCs, which could be also proved by the further increase of the hydrodynamic diameter of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ caused by Fe3+ (Fig. S2). Therefore, we concluded that the fluorescence quenching of BSA-CuNCs was attributable to the Fe3+-triggered aggregation induced quenching effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-integrated POCT platform for onsite determination of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Having profiled the principle of BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ responding to Fe3+, we subsequently integrated it into smartphone for enabling an intelligent POCT platform toward Fe3+. To this end, we took a small dark cavity as the carrier to equip with smartphone, a sample slot to load filter paper strip, a long-pass filter, and a miniature 365 nm UV lamp that linked to mobile power (Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. 5A, the smartphone camera was used to gather a series of fluorescence images with a successive variation from cyan-blue to red with the Fe3+ concentration from 0.0–250.0 μΜ. Afterward, these fluorescence images were analyzed through the Color Picker APP, obtaining the Fe3+ concentration-dependent RGB values. Finally, the linear relationship expressed as R/B = 0.0202 [Fe3+] + 0.667 (R 2 = 0.995) with a LOD of 0.086 μM were obtained over the Fe3+ concentration range from 0.0 to 15.0 μM (Fig. 5B). Comparing with other covered smartphone-integrated platforms (Table S3), the LOD of the BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+-integrated smartphone platform for Fe3+ monitoring was lower, which was also much below than the WHO guidelines of 5.357 μM in drinking water [47]. All these results revealed the great potential of this smartphone-assisted POCT platform for onsite visual quantitative determination of Fe3+.

Having manifested the available sensitivity of this proposed smartphone-assisted POCT platform for Fe3+, we next tested its selectivity for onsite visual monitoring of Fe3+. Under the same conditions, the color of the built-in strip changed from cyan-blue to red, and the R/B value was 2.0-fold increased when 100.0 μM Fe3+ was added. However, there was no corresponding change when other interfering ions were added (Fig. 5C). These results validated that the high selective detection ability of this BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+-integrated smartphone-based POCT platform for Fe3+, encouraging us for the following study of its practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Onsite visual POC assay of Fe3+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicability of this portable BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+-integrated smartphone-based POCT platform for Fe3+ in real samples, we explored its response ability toward Fe3+ in tap water and commercial spirits through the standard addition way. Table 1 shown that he satisfactory recoveries of Fe3+ were obtained from 98.54% to 106.63%, which were in line with the results got by ICP-MS for all the samples, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) was lower than 5%, indicating an excellent repetitiveness for this POCT device for the onsite visual determination of Fe3+ in actual samples. The observations described herein well unveiled that our proposed portable POCT platform was feasible to meet the onsite quantitative measuring requirement of Fe3+ in real samples.

Summarily, we have exploited an intelligent, rapid, reliable and portable POCT platform for Fe3+ detection in tap water and commercial spirits. Owing to Fe3+-triggered aggregation-caused quenching of BSA-CuNCs and [Ru(bpy)3]2+ as an internal signal, BSA-CuNCs@ [Ru(bpy)3]2+ showed the ratiometric emission answering toward Fe3+. Through integration with the smartphone, Fe3+ concentration-dependent fluorescence color images were observed on the smartphone-based POCT platform, which could be ultimately converted into RGB values with its built-in Color Picker APP. By plotting R/B value versus Fe3+ concentration, a POC assay for onsite rapid quantitative determination of Fe3+ was proposed in commercial spirits and tap water samples with a high accuracy comparable to that of classical ICP-MS, revealing a great practical potential of this proposed POCT gadget for onsite visual monitoring of Fe3+ in the environment and food matrices. This work provided a good example for developing efficient, reliable, and low-cost POCT platform, which might be extended to other potential contaminants for establishing efficient warning-early mechanisms to safeguard food safety and environmental health.

Ying Wang: Data curation, Validation, Writing − original draft. Jiayu Jian: Data curation, Writing − original draft. Baoguo Sun: Supervision. Yunlong Wei: Data curation. Daodong Pan: Supervision. Jinxuan Cao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing − review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yizhong Shen: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing − review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research Program of China (2016YFD0401502), the Modern Agricultural Technical Foundation of China (CARS-42–25), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22176047), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (PA2021GDSK0101).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132413.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Cotruvo, 2017 WHO Guidelines for Drinking Water Quality: First Addendum to the Fourth Edition, J. Am. Water Works Ass., 109 (2017) 44–51.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ying Wang: Associate Professor, Master's tutor, Beijing Technology and Business University. Research interests: environmental monitoring.
Jiayu Jian: Studied in Ningbo University for Master’s degree from 2019 till now. Research interests: the synthesis of nanomaterials and food safety analysis.
Baoguo Sun: Professor, Doctor's tutor, Beijing Technology and Business University. Research interests: food spice and Baijiu flavor.
Yunlong Wei: Studied in Hefei University of Technology for Master’s degree from 2020 till now. Research interests: the synthesis of nanomaterials and food safety analysis.
Daodong Pan: Professor, Doctor's tutor, Ningbo University. Research interests: meat processing and quality control, lactic acid bacteria and their functional factors.
Jinxuan Cao: Professor, Doctor's tutor, Beijing Technology and Business University. Research interests: food safety analysis, meat quality and flavor.
Yizhong Shen: Associate Professor, Master's tutor, Hefei University of Technology. Research interests: analytical chemistry for life sciences.
